docs.title: docsync
docs.text:
Keep your code and documentation in sync with centralized linking and section-aware staleness detection.

docsync is a pre-commit hook and CLI tool that ensures documentation stays fresh by tracking relationships between code files and their documentation.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Centralized TOML links - Define code↔doc relationships in .docsync/links.toml (no inline comments!)
  • Section-level precision - Link to specific doc sections (docs/api.md#Authentication) for targeted staleness checking
  • Git diff-based staleness - Uses git diff to detect if doc sections were updated since code changed
  • Import-aware staleness - Detects when docs are stale due to transitive code changes
  • Pre-commit enforcement - Blocks commits when documentation is missing or outdated
  • Agent-native UX - JSON output, deterministic checks, and scoped update tasks for AI agents
  • Auto-bootstrap - Intelligently generates links from filename conventions and content analysis
  • Doc audit skill - Claude Code skill that analyzes docs for trackability

docs.title: Quick Install
docs.text:
# Install with uv (recommended)
uv pip install git+https://github.com/nlebovits/docsync.git

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
docsync provides deterministic staleness detection that enables scoped, reliable updates:

  1. You define relationships in .docsync/links.toml: 

    [[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["docs/api.md#Authentication"]

  2. docsync detects staleness via git diff analysis: 

    $ git commit -m "refactor: extract auth helpers"

docsync: ❌ commit blocked

Stale documentation detected:
  docs/api.md#Authentication
    Code: src/auth.py
    Reason: Section unchanged since src/auth.py changed

  3. You update the precise section - no guessing, no reading 500-line files:

  4. Update lines 45-89 in docs/api.md (the Authentication section)
  5. Don't touch other sections

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Getting Started - Installation and setup
  • Tutorial - Real-world onboarding workflow
  • Concepts - How docsync works
  • CLI Reference - All commands

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: This document describes docsync's internal architecture and core modules.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
docsync is built around a bidirectional graph that maps code files to documentation targets.

The graph enables:

  • Fast lookups: "Which docs link to this code file?"
  • Reverse lookups: "Which code files link to this doc?"
  • Transitive staleness: "Did any imported code change?"

docs.title: File Structure
docs.text:
.docsync/
├── links.toml          # Source of truth for code↔doc links (COMMIT THIS)
└── cache/              # Import graph cache (IGNORE THIS)
    └── imports_*.json

pyproject.toml          # [tool.docsync] configuration

docs.title: What to Commit
docs.text:
DO commit: - .docsync/links.toml - Shared source of truth - pyproject.toml - Project configuration

DON'T commit: - .docsync/cache/ - Temporary import graph cache

Add to .gitignore: 

.docsync/cache/

docs.title: Core Modules
docs.text: Module Purpose cli.py Command-line interface and skill discovery config.py Configuration parsing from pyproject.toml toml_links.py TOML link file parsing and graph construction sections.py Markdown section parsing and line range extraction staleness.py Git diff-based section staleness detection graph.py Bidirectional graph construction imports.py Python import graph via AST parsing hook.py Pre-commit hook entry point donttouch.py Protected content guard coverage.py Documentation coverage reporting cache.py Import graph caching

docs.title: Graph Structure
docs.text: The docsync graph is a bidirectional mapping between code files and doc targets.

docs.title: Example Graph
docs.text:
Given these links:

[[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["docs/api.md#Authentication", "docs/auth.md"]

[[link]]
code = "src/models/user.py"
docs = ["docs/models.md#User Model"]

The graph is:

{
  # Code → Docs
  "src/auth.py": {
    "docs/api.md#Authentication",
    "docs/auth.md"
  },
  "src/models/user.py": {
    "docs/models.md#User Model"
  },

  # Docs → Code (reverse)
  "docs/api.md#Authentication": {"src/auth.py"},
  "docs/auth.md": {"src/auth.py"},
  "docs/models.md#User Model": {"src/models/user.py"},
}

docs.title: Section-Specific Targets
docs.text:
Section targets (docs/api.md#Authentication) are distinct nodes from whole-file targets (docs/api.md).

This enables targeted staleness checking - only the specific section needs updating, not the entire file.

docs.title: Section Parsing
docs.text: The sections.py module extracts markdown section headings and their line ranges.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def extract_sections(markdown_path):
    """Extract sections with their line ranges."""
    sections = []
    current_section = None

    for line_num, line in enumerate(read_file(markdown_path)):
        if line.startswith('#'):
            # Close previous section
            if current_section:
                current_section['end'] = line_num - 1
                sections.append(current_section)

            # Start new section
            heading = line.lstrip('#').strip()
            current_section = {
                'heading': heading,
                'start': line_num,
            }

    # Close final section
    if current_section:
        current_section['end'] = line_num
        sections.append(current_section)

    return sections

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Given docs/api.md:

# API Documentation

Introduction text...

## Authentication

Auth details (lines 5-30)

## Session Management

Session details (lines 32-60)

Output:

[
  {'heading': 'API Documentation', 'start': 0, 'end': 3},
  {'heading': 'Authentication', 'start': 4, 'end': 30},
  {'heading': 'Session Management', 'start': 31, 'end': 60},
]

docs.title: Staleness Detection
docs.text: The staleness.py module implements git diff-based staleness checking.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def is_doc_stale(code_file, doc_target):
    """Check if a doc target is stale."""
    # 1. Get last commit that changed the code file
    last_commit = git_last_commit(code_file)

    # 2. Run git diff from that commit to HEAD
    diff = git_diff(last_commit, 'HEAD', doc_target.file)

    # 3. Parse diff to get changed line numbers
    changed_lines = parse_diff_lines(diff)

    # 4. If target is section-specific, check line range overlap
    if doc_target.section:
        section_range = get_section_range(doc_target.file, doc_target.section)
        if any(line in section_range for line in changed_lines):
            return False  # Section was updated, not stale
        else:
            return True   # Section unchanged, stale
    else:
        # Whole-file target
        if changed_lines:
            return False  # File was updated
        else:
            return True   # File unchanged

docs.title: Why Git Diff?
docs.text:
Alternatives considered:

  • File modification time - unreliable (git operations change mtime)
  • Line-by-line comparison - doesn't capture "when was this written"
  • Manual timestamps - error-prone, not version-controlled

Git diff wins because:

  • Version-controlled (part of git history)
  • Precise (line-level granularity)
  • Fast (native git operation)
  • Deterministic (same result every time)

docs.title: Import Graph
docs.text: The imports.py module builds a Python import graph via AST parsing.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Enable transitive staleness detection:

  • src/auth.py imports src/crypto.py
  • src/crypto.py changed
  • Therefore docs for src/auth.py may be stale

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def build_import_graph(repo_root):
    """Build import graph via AST parsing."""
    graph = {}

    for py_file in find_python_files(repo_root):
        imports = extract_imports_ast(py_file)
        graph[py_file] = resolve_imports(imports, repo_root)

    return graph

docs.title: Caching
docs.text:
Import graphs are expensive to compute (AST parsing for every Python file).

The cache.py module caches graphs using:

  • SHA-based invalidation - Cache key includes file content hash
  • Automatic cleanup - Old cache entries removed on first miss

Cache location: .docsync/cache/imports_<sha>.json

docs.title: Protected Content
docs.text: The donttouch.py module guards critical content from staleness checks.

docs.title: Data Structure
docs.text:
{
  'sections': [
    'README.md#License',
    'docs/contributing.md#Code of Conduct',
  ],
  'literals': [
    'Python 3.11+',
    '#7730E1',
  ],
}

docs.title: Enforcement
docs.text:
When checking staleness:

if doc_target in protected_sections:
    return False  # Never stale, skip check

When validating changes:

if literal in protected_literals and literal_changed:
    warn(f"Protected literal changed: {literal}")

docs.title: Coverage Calculation
docs.text: The coverage.py module reports documentation coverage.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def calculate_coverage(graph, config):
    """Calculate documentation coverage."""
    code_files = find_code_files(config.require_links, config.exempt)
    documented = [f for f in code_files if f in graph]

    coverage = len(documented) / len(code_files)

    return {
        'coverage': coverage,
        'total_files': len(code_files),
        'documented_files': len(documented),
        'undocumented_files': [f for f in code_files if f not in documented],
    }

docs.title: What Counts as "Documented"
docs.text:
A code file is documented if:

  • It appears as a code field in any [[link]] entry
  • Or matches a glob pattern in a code field

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Contributing - Development setup
  • Concepts - Understand the graph model

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: docsync is configured via [tool.docsync] in pyproject.toml.

docs.title: Default Configuration
docs.text:
Created by docsync init:

[tool.docsync]
mode = "block"
transitive_depth = 1
enforce_symmetry = true
require_links = []
exempt = []
doc_paths = ["docs/**/*.md", "README.md"]

docs.title: mode
docs.text:
Type: str Default: "block" Values: "block" | "warn"

Controls enforcement behavior:

  • block - Commits are blocked when docs are stale (exit code 1)
  • warn - Print warnings but allow commits (exit code 0)

Example:

[tool.docsync]
mode = "warn"  # Non-blocking for initial onboarding

When to use warn: Onboarding a legacy project with lots of stale docs. Gradually increase coverage before switching to block.

docs.title: transitive_depth
docs.text:
Type: int Default: 1 Values: 0 | 1 | 2 | ...

How deep to check imports for transitive staleness:

  • 0 - Disable transitive staleness (only direct code→doc links)
  • 1 - Check direct imports only
  • 2 - Check imports of imports
  • Higher - Exponentially expensive, rarely useful

Example:

[tool.docsync]
transitive_depth = 0  # Disable transitive checks

Trade-off:

  • Higher depth = more accurate staleness detection
  • Higher depth = slower performance

Recommendation: Start with 1. Increase only if you miss important transitive staleness.

docs.title: enforce_symmetry
docs.text:
Type: bool Default: true

Whether to require symmetric links (code→doc implies doc→code).

Example:

[tool.docsync]
enforce_symmetry = false  # Allow one-way links

Effect of true:

If src/auth.py links to docs/api.md, then docs/api.md must also link back to src/auth.py (in the graph representation).

When to disable: Rarely. Symmetry ensures graph consistency.

docs.title: require_links
docs.text:
Type: list[str] Default: []

Glob patterns for code files that must have documentation links.

Example:

[tool.docsync]
require_links = ["src/**/*.py"]  # All source files must be documented

Effect:

Files matching these patterns will trigger errors if not linked to any docs.

Use cases:

  • Enforce documentation coverage for production code
  • Exclude tests, scripts, experiments from requirement

Example with exclusions:

[tool.docsync]
require_links = ["src/**/*.py"]
exempt = ["src/experimental/**", "src/legacy/**"]

docs.title: exempt
docs.text:
Type: list[str] Default: []

Glob patterns for code files to ignore entirely.

Example:

[tool.docsync]
exempt = [
  "tests/**",
  "scripts/**",
  "src/experimental/**",
]

Effect:

Files matching these patterns:

  • Are not counted in coverage
  • Don't trigger staleness checks
  • Can't be linked in .docsync/links.toml

Use cases:

  • Exclude test files
  • Exclude scripts and tooling
  • Exclude experimental code

docs.title: doc_paths
docs.text:
Type: list[str] Default: ["docs/**/*.md", "README.md"]

Glob patterns for files that are considered "documentation."

Example:

[tool.docsync]
doc_paths = [
  "docs/**/*.md",
  "README.md",
  "CHANGELOG.md",
  "CLAUDE.md",
  "*.rst",  # Include reStructuredText
]

Effect:

Only files matching these patterns can be link targets.

Use cases:

  • Add reStructuredText (.rst) files
  • Include CLAUDE.md for AI agent instructions
  • Include CHANGELOG.md for version history

docs.title: Strict (Production)
docs.text:
[tool.docsync]
mode = "block"
transitive_depth = 1
enforce_symmetry = true
require_links = ["src/**/*.py"]  # All source files must be documented
exempt = ["tests/**", "scripts/**"]
doc_paths = ["docs/**/*.md", "README.md", "CLAUDE.md"]

All source code must have docs. Commits blocked on staleness.

docs.title: Permissive (Onboarding)
docs.text:
[tool.docsync]
mode = "warn"  # Non-blocking
transitive_depth = 0  # Disable transitive checks
enforce_symmetry = false
require_links = []  # No coverage requirements
exempt = ["tests/**", "scripts/**", "src/legacy/**"]
doc_paths = ["docs/**/*.md", "README.md"]

Warnings only. Good for gradual adoption.

docs.title: Hybrid (Incremental)
docs.text:
[tool.docsync]
mode = "block"
transitive_depth = 1
enforce_symmetry = true
require_links = [
  "src/core/**/*.py",  # Core modules must be documented
  "src/api/**/*.py",   # API modules must be documented
]
exempt = [
  "tests/**",
  "scripts/**",
  "src/experimental/**",
  "src/legacy/**",
]
doc_paths = ["docs/**/*.md", "README.md", "CLAUDE.md"]

Enforce docs for critical modules. Exempt legacy and experimental code.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• CLI Reference - Command options
  • Getting Started - Setup walkthrough

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to docsync!

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.11+ required
  • uv package manager (recommended)
  • git for version control

docs.title: Clone and Install
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/nlebovits/docsync.git
cd docsync

# Install dependencies with uv
uv sync

# Verify installation
uv run docsync --help

docs.title: Run Tests
docs.text:
uv run pytest

With coverage:

uv run pytest --cov=docsync --cov-report=term-missing

docs.title: Linting and Formatting
docs.text:
# Check code style
uv run ruff check .

# Format code
uv run ruff format .

Fix linting issues automatically:

uv run ruff check --fix .

docs.title: Code Style
docs.text:
• Formatter: ruff (line length: 100)
  • Linter: ruff (select: E, W, F, I, UP, B, SIM, N)
  • Type hints: Encouraged but not required

docs.title: Commit Convention
docs.text:
This project uses Conventional Commits:

git commit -m "feat(cli): add validate-links command"
git commit -m "fix(staleness): handle missing sections gracefully"
git commit -m "docs: update README with section-level examples"

Types:

  • feat: - New feature
  • fix: - Bug fix
  • docs: - Documentation changes
  • test: - Test additions or fixes
  • refactor: - Code restructuring
  • chore: - Maintenance tasks

Scopes (optional):

  • cli - CLI commands
  • graph - Graph construction
  • staleness - Staleness detection
  • sections - Section parsing

docs.title: Versioning
docs.text:
docsync uses commitizen for semantic versioning.

# Bump version and create changelog
uv run cz bump

# Bump to specific version
uv run cz bump --increment MAJOR|MINOR|PATCH

Version bumps are automated based on conventional commit messages:

  • feat: → MINOR version bump
  • fix: → PATCH version bump
  • BREAKING CHANGE: → MAJOR version bump

docs.title: Running Specific Tests
docs.text:
# Run a single test file
uv run pytest tests/test_staleness.py

# Run tests matching a pattern
uv run pytest -k "test_section"

docs.title: Writing Tests
docs.text:
Place tests in tests/ directory, mirroring src/docsync/ structure:

tests/
├── test_cli.py          # Tests for src/docsync/cli.py
├── test_staleness.py    # Tests for src/docsync/staleness.py
└── test_sections.py     # Tests for src/docsync/sections.py

Use pytest fixtures for common setup:

import pytest
from pathlib import Path

@pytest.fixture
def sample_repo(tmp_path):
    """Create a sample git repository for testing."""
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    # ... setup git repo ...
    return repo

def test_staleness_detection(sample_repo):
    """Test staleness detection in a controlled repo."""
    # ... test implementation ...

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
When adding a new feature:

  • [ ] Write tests first (TDD encouraged)
  • [ ] Implement the feature
  • [ ] Update documentation (README.md, docs/)
  • [ ] Add CLI command if applicable
  • [ ] Update CHANGELOG.md (automated via cz bump)
  • [ ] Ensure tests pass: uv run pytest
  • [ ] Ensure linting passes: uv run ruff check .
  • [ ] Use conventional commit message

docs.title: Example: Adding a New Command
docs.text:
1. Add argparse subcommand in src/docsync/cli.py:
# In main()
new_parser = subparsers.add_parser(
    "new-command",
    help="Description of new command"
)
new_parser.add_argument("--option", help="Optional argument")
  1. Implement handler:
def handle_new_command(args, config):
    """Handle the new command."""
    # ... implementation ...
    return 0  # Success
  1. Wire up in main():
elif args.command == "new-command":
    return handle_new_command(args, config)
  1. Write tests:
def test_new_command(sample_repo):
    result = run_cli(["new-command", "--option", "value"])
    assert result.exit_code == 0
  1. Update docs:

Add entry to docs/cli/reference.md.

docs.title: Pull Request Process
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository

  2. Create a feature branch:

git checkout -b feat/your-feature-name

  1. Make changes following project standards

  2. Run tests and linting:

uv run pytest
uv run ruff check .
uv run ruff format .
  1. Commit with conventional message:
git commit -m "feat(cli): add new-command for X"
  1. Push to your fork:
git push origin feat/your-feature-name

  1. Open a Pull Request against main branch

  2. Address review feedback

docs.title: Code of Conduct
docs.text:
Please be respectful and inclusive in all interactions.

Harassment, discrimination, or abusive behavior will not be tolerated.

docs.title: License
docs.text: By contributing to docsync, you agree that your contributions will be licensed under the Apache-2.0 License.

docs.title: Questions?
docs.text:
• Issues: GitHub Issues
  • Discussions: GitHub Discussions

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Architecture - Understand the codebase
  • CLI Reference - Command documentation

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide walks you through setting up docsync in your project.

docs.title: Using uv (Recommended)
docs.text: uv pip install git+https://github.com/nlebovits/docsync.git

docs.title: Using pip
docs.text: pip install git+https://github.com/nlebovits/docsync.git

docs.title: Initialize in Your Repository
docs.text:
Navigate to your repository and run:

cd your-repo
docsync init

This creates:

  • [tool.docsync] configuration in pyproject.toml
  • .docsync/links.toml file for defining code-doc links
  • .docsync/ directory for cache storage

docs.title: Define Code-Documentation Links
docs.text: Edit .docsync/links.toml to establish relationships between code and docs:

docs.title: Simple Whole-File Link
docs.text:
[[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["docs/api.md"]

When src/auth.py changes, docs/api.md must be updated.

docs.title: Section-Specific Link (Recommended)
docs.text:
[[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["docs/api.md#Authentication"]

More precise! Only the Authentication section needs updating when src/auth.py changes.

docs.title: Multiple Targets
docs.text:
[[link]]
code = "src/models/user.py"
docs = [
  "docs/models.md#User Model",
  "docs/api.md#User Endpoints",
]

One code file can link to multiple doc sections.

docs.title: Glob Patterns
docs.text:
[[link]]
code = "src/models/*.py"
docs = ["docs/models.md"]

All Python files in src/models/ link to docs/models.md.

docs.title: Auto-Generate Links
docs.text:
Instead of writing links manually, use bootstrap:

docsync bootstrap --apply

This intelligently proposes links using:

  • Filename matching: src/auth.py → docs/auth.md
  • Content analysis: Grep docs for references to code files
  • Import graph: Transitive link suggestions

Warning

Bootstrap can't reliably infer links for human-facing docs (tutorials, examples, READMEs). Review all suggestions carefully.

docs.title: Install the Pre-Commit Hook
docs.text:
docsync install-hook

Now commits will be blocked if documentation is stale:

$ git add src/auth.py
$ git commit -m "refactor: extract auth helpers"

docsync: ❌ commit blocked

Stale documentation detected:
  docs/api.md#Authentication
    Code: src/auth.py
    Reason: Section unchanged since src/auth.py changed

docs.title: Check All Links Are Valid
docs.text:
docsync validate-links

Expected output: 

✓ All 10 links are valid

docs.title: Check Documentation Coverage
docs.text:
docsync coverage

Expected output: 

Documentation Coverage: 85.0%
  Total code files: 20
  Documented: 17
  Undocumented files:
    src/experimental.py
    src/legacy.py
    src/draft.py

docs.title: What's Next?
docs.text:
• Tutorial - See a real-world onboarding workflow
  • Concepts - Understand how links work
  • CLI Reference - Explore all commands
  • Configuration - Customize docsync behavior

docs.title: Tutorial: Real-World Onboarding Workflow
docs.text:
This tutorial shows the complete docsync onboarding workflow based on an actual session onboarding portolan-cli to docsync.

You'll see every command, the issues encountered, and how they were resolved.

docs.title: Step 1: Initialize docsync
docs.text:
$ docsync init
✓ Added [tool.docsync] to pyproject.toml
✓ Created .docsync/
✓ Created .docsync/links.toml

docs.title: Issue Encountered
docs.text:
The default config used src/**/*.py but the project uses portolan_cli/. Had to manually fix:

# pyproject.toml - before (wrong)
[tool.docsync]
require_links = ["src/**/*.py"]

# pyproject.toml - after (correct)
[tool.docsync]
require_links = ["portolan_cli/**/*.py"]

Note

This issue is tracked in #9. Future versions will auto-detect the source directory from pyproject.toml.

docs.title: Step 2: Check Initial Coverage
docs.text:
$ docsync coverage
Documentation Coverage: 0.0%
  Total code files: 60
  Documented: 0

No links defined yet, so coverage is 0%.

docs.title: Step 3: Create Initial links.toml
docs.text:
Identified key code→doc relationships:

# .docsync/links.toml

# CLI reference (auto-generated via mkdocs-click)
[[link]]
code = "portolan_cli/cli.py"
docs = ["docs/reference/cli.md"]

# Configuration docs
[[link]]
code = "portolan_cli/config.py"
docs = ["docs/reference/configuration.md"]

[[link]]
code = "portolan_cli/conversion_config.py"
docs = ["docs/reference/configuration.md#Conversion Configuration"]

# Output module documented in contributing guide
[[link]]
code = "portolan_cli/output.py"
docs = ["docs/contributing.md#Code Standards"]

# Core modules linked to design principles
[[link]]
code = "portolan_cli/scan.py"
docs = ["CLAUDE.md#Design Principles"]

docs.title: Step 4: Validate and Check Coverage
docs.text:
$ docsync validate-links
✓ All 10 links are valid

$ docsync coverage
Documentation Coverage: 16.7%
  Total code files: 60
  Documented: 10

Coverage improved from 0% to 16.7% with 10 links.

docs.title: Step 5: Run the Audit Skill
docs.text:
Following .claude/skills/audit.md, analyzed each doc file for:

  • Structure - Headings, tables, code blocks
  • File references - Backtick-quoted paths
  • Section scope - Does each section map to code?
  • Protected content - Licenses, terminology

docs.title: Audit Findings
docs.text:
The audit skill identified:

  1. Suggested links from file path mentions:
  2. portolan_cli/output.py mentioned in CLAUDE.md#Standardized Terminal Output

  3. portolan_cli/json_output.py referenced in docs/reference/cli.md

  4. Protected sections to add to .docsync/donttouch:

  5. License sections (must not change without decision)
  6. Brand colors in BRANDING.md

  7. Python version requirement

  8. Restructuring suggestions:

  9. Convert command flag table to structured format
  10. Split large architecture section into subsections

docs.title: Step 6: Create donttouch Protections
docs.text:
$ cat .docsync/donttouch
# License sections must not change without explicit decision
README.md#License
docs/contributing.md#License

# Code of Conduct is policy
docs/contributing.md#Code of Conduct

# Terminology is enforced project-wide
CLAUDE.md#Terminology (ENFORCED)

# Brand colors must be consistent
docs/BRANDING.md#Color Palette

# Version requirements
"Python 3.10+"

These sections and literals are now protected from accidental changes.

docs.title: Step 7: Add Audit-Suggested Links
docs.text:
# Additional links from audit

[[link]]
code = "portolan_cli/output.py"
docs = ["CLAUDE.md#Standardized Terminal Output"]

[[link]]
code = "portolan_cli/json_output.py"
docs = ["CLAUDE.md#Design Principles"]

[[link]]
code = "portolan_cli/status.py"
docs = ["docs/reference/cli.md"]

# ... 6 more links

docs.title: Step 8: Final Coverage
docs.text:
$ docsync coverage
Documentation Coverage: 26.7%
  Total code files: 60
  Documented: 16

$ docsync list-protected
Protected sections:
  README.md#License
  docs/contributing.md#License
  docs/contributing.md#Code of Conduct
  CLAUDE.md#Terminology (ENFORCED)
  docs/BRANDING.md#Color Palette

Global literals:
  "Python 3.10+"

Coverage improved from 16.7% to 26.7% with audit-suggested links.

docs.title: Step 9: Add Pre-Commit Hook
docs.text:
# .pre-commit-config.yaml
- id: docsync-check
  name: check docs freshness
  entry: bash -c '$HOME/.local/bin/docsync check'
  language: system
  files: ^(portolan_cli/.*\.py|docs/.*\.md|CLAUDE\.md)$
  pass_filenames: false

Note

Had to use bash -c '$HOME/...' workaround due to pyenv shim issues - see #10.

docs.title: Step 10: Test the Workflow
docs.text:
Made a change to portolan_cli/cli.py:

$ git add portolan_cli/cli.py
$ git commit -m "feat: add --verbose flag"

docsync: ❌ commit blocked

Stale documentation detected:
  docs/reference/cli.md
    Code: portolan_cli/cli.py
    Reason: File unchanged since portolan_cli/cli.py changed

Updated docs/reference/cli.md (mkdocs-click auto-generates from docstrings), then:

$ git add docs/reference/cli.md
$ git commit -m "feat: add --verbose flag"
✓ docsync check passed

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Starting from 0% coverage, the workflow achieved:

  • 26.7% coverage (16 of 60 files documented)
  • Protected content via .docsync/donttouch
  • Pre-commit enforcement via hook
  • Audit-driven improvements from Claude Code skill

This demonstrates docsync's value: deterministic staleness detection enables reliable, scoped documentation updates.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Concepts - Understand how links work
  • Configuration - Customize docsync behavior
  • Skills - Learn about the audit skill

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: Complete reference for all docsync commands.

docs.title: Global Options
docs.text:
All commands support these options:

  • --help - Show help message and exit

docs.title: init
docs.text:
Initialize docsync configuration in your repository.

docsync init

Effect:

  • Adds [tool.docsync] to pyproject.toml
  • Creates .docsync/ directory
  • Creates .docsync/links.toml file

When to use: First time setting up docsync in a project.

docs.title: install-hook
docs.text:
Install the pre-commit hook.

docsync install-hook

Effect:

  • Creates .git/hooks/pre-commit script
  • Hook runs docsync check before each commit

When to use: After docsync init to enable enforcement.

docs.title: bootstrap
docs.text:
Auto-generate links from conventions and content analysis.

docsync bootstrap [--apply]

Options:

  • --apply - Apply proposed links to .docsync/links.toml

Without --apply: Shows proposed links for review.

With --apply: Writes links to .docsync/links.toml.

How it works:

  1. Filename matching - src/auth.py → docs/auth.md
  2. Content analysis - Grep docs for code file references
  3. Import graph - Suggest transitive links

Example:

$ docsync bootstrap

Proposed 15 links:

  src/auth.py
    → docs/api.md
    → examples/quickstart.md#Login Flow  ⚠️

⚠️  Warning: 1 doc may be tutorial/guide (marked with ⚠️)
Review these carefully - automated links may not make sense for narrative docs.

To apply these links, run:
  docsync bootstrap --apply

When to use: Initial link generation or discovering missing links.

docs.title: validate-links
docs.text:
Validate that all link references exist.

docsync validate-links

Checks:

  • Code files exist
  • Doc files exist
  • Sections exist in doc files

Example output:

✓ All 15 links are valid

Or with errors: 

❌ Invalid links:
  src/auth.py → docs/api.md#NonExistent
    Section 'NonExistent' not found in docs/api.md

  src/missing.py → docs/api.md
    Code file 'src/missing.py' does not exist

When to use: After editing .docsync/links.toml, before committing.

docs.title: coverage
docs.text:
Report documentation coverage.

docsync coverage [--format text|json]

Options:

  • --format - Output format (default: text)

Example output (text):

Documentation Coverage: 85.0%
  Total code files: 20
  Documented: 17
  Undocumented files:
    src/experimental.py
    src/legacy.py
    src/draft.py

Example output (json):

{
  "coverage": 0.85,
  "total_files": 20,
  "documented_files": 17,
  "undocumented_files": [
    "src/experimental.py",
    "src/legacy.py",
    "src/draft.py"
  ]
}

When to use: Audits, reporting, tracking coverage over time.

docs.title: check
docs.text:
Check staged files for doc freshness (CI/pre-commit).

docsync check [--staged-files FILES] [--format text|json]

Options:

  • --staged-files - Comma-separated list of files (overrides git staged)
  • --format - Output format (default: text)

Purpose: Fast, focused check for pre-commit hooks and CI pipelines.

Scope: Only examines docs linked to staged files (or specified files).

Example (text):

$ docsync check

docsync: ❌ commit blocked

Stale documentation detected:
  docs/api.md#Authentication
    Code: src/auth.py
    Reason: Section unchanged since src/auth.py changed

Example (json):

{
  "stale": [
    {
      "code_file": "src/auth.py",
      "doc_target": "docs/api.md#Authentication",
      "section": "Authentication",
      "reason": "Section unchanged since src/auth.py changed"
    }
  ]
}

Exit codes:

  • 0 - All docs fresh
  • 1 - Stale docs found

When to use: Pre-commit hooks, CI pipelines, focused checks.

docs.title: list-stale
docs.text:
List ALL stale docs regardless of recent changes (audit).

docsync list-stale [--format text|paths|json]

Options:

  • --format - Output format (default: text)
  • text - Human-readable
  • paths - Just file paths (one per line)
  • json - Machine-readable

Purpose: Comprehensive audit of documentation staleness.

Scope: Scans the entire repository for any stale docs.

Example (text):

$ docsync list-stale

Stale documentation:

  docs/api.md#Authentication
    Code: src/auth.py
    Reason: Section unchanged since src/auth.py changed

  docs/models.md
    Code: src/models/user.py
    Reason: File unchanged since src/models/user.py changed

Example (paths):

docs/api.md
docs/models.md

Example (json):

{
  "stale": [
    {
      "code_file": "src/auth.py",
      "doc_target": "docs/api.md#Authentication",
      "section": "Authentication",
      "reason": "Section unchanged since src/auth.py changed"
    }
  ]
}

When to use: Periodic audits, reviews, finding all stale docs.

docs.title: affected-docs
docs.text:
Show docs affected by code changes.

docsync affected-docs --files FILE1,FILE2,... [--format text|json]

Options:

  • --files - Comma-separated list of code files
  • --format - Output format (default: text)

Purpose: Discover which docs need updating when code changes.

Example (text):

$ docsync affected-docs --files src/auth.py,src/crypto.py

Affected documentation:

  src/auth.py:
    → docs/api.md#Authentication
    → docs/architecture.md#Auth Module

  src/crypto.py:
    → docs/architecture.md#Crypto Module
    → docs/api.md#Session Management (transitive)

Example (json):

{
  "affected_docs": {
    "src/auth.py": [
      "docs/api.md#Authentication",
      "docs/architecture.md#Auth Module"
    ],
    "src/crypto.py": [
      "docs/architecture.md#Crypto Module",
      "docs/api.md#Session Management"
    ]
  }
}

When to use: Planning updates, understanding impact of changes.

docs.title: info
docs.text:
Show all links for a file.

docsync info FILE [--format text|json]

Options:

  • --format - Output format (default: text)

Example (text):

$ docsync info src/auth.py

Links for src/auth.py:

  Documentation:
    → docs/api.md#Authentication
    → docs/architecture.md#Auth Module
    → CLAUDE.md#Authentication Patterns

  Imported by:
    ← src/api/routes.py
    ← src/cli/login.py

Example (json):

{
  "file": "src/auth.py",
  "docs": [
    "docs/api.md#Authentication",
    "docs/architecture.md#Auth Module",
    "CLAUDE.md#Authentication Patterns"
  ],
  "imported_by": [
    "src/api/routes.py",
    "src/cli/login.py"
  ]
}

When to use: Understanding relationships, debugging links.

docs.title: list-protected
docs.text:
List protected sections and literals.

docsync list-protected [--format text|json]

Options:

  • --format - Output format (default: text)

Example (text):

Protected sections:
  README.md#License
  docs/contributing.md#Code of Conduct
  CLAUDE.md#Terminology

Global literals:
  "Python 3.11+"
  "#7730E1"

Example (json):

{
  "protected_sections": [
    "README.md#License",
    "docs/contributing.md#Code of Conduct"
  ],
  "protected_literals": [
    "Python 3.11+",
    "#7730E1"
  ]
}

When to use: Auditing protected content, understanding policies.

docs.title: skills
docs.text:
List available Claude Code skills.

docsync skills

Example output:

Available Claude Code skills:

  audit - Analyze documentation for trackability
    Location: .claude/skills/audit.md
    Usage: Ask Claude to "audit the documentation"

When to use: Discovering available skills, integration with Claude Code.

docs.title: clear-cache
docs.text:
Clear the import graph cache.

docsync clear-cache

Effect: Deletes .docsync/cache/ directory.

When to use: After major refactoring, if cache seems stale.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Configuration - Customize command behavior
  • Getting Started - Setup walkthrough

docs.title: Protected Content
docs.text: The .docsync/donttouch file protects critical content from accidental changes.

docs.title: Why Protected Content?
docs.text:
Some documentation content is policy, not implementation details:

  • Licenses - Legal text that must not change without review
  • Version requirements - "Python 3.11+" is a project constraint
  • Brand assets - Color palettes, logos must stay consistent
  • Code of Conduct - Community policy
  • Terminology - Enforced project-wide naming conventions

These should never be marked as stale just because related code changed.

docs.title: Format
docs.text: The .docsync/donttouch file has two types of protections:

docs.title: Section Protection
docs.text:
Protect specific doc sections:

# Comments are allowed
README.md#License
docs/contributing.md#Code of Conduct
CLAUDE.md#Terminology (ENFORCED)

Effect: docsync will never mark these sections as stale, even if linked code changes.

docs.title: Literal Protection
docs.text:
Protect specific strings across all docs:

"Python 3.11+"
"Apache-2.0"
"#7730E1"

Effect: docsync will warn if these literals are changed.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Given this .docsync/donttouch:

# License sections are legal text
README.md#License
docs/contributing.md#License

# Brand colors must match BRANDING.md
docs/index.md#Colors
mkdocs.yml#palette

# Version requirement is a constraint
"Python 3.11+"

docs.title: Scenario 1: Section Protection
docs.text:
# .docsync/links.toml
[[link]]
code = "setup.py"
docs = ["README.md#License"]

Even when setup.py changes, README.md#License is never marked stale (it's protected).

docs.title: Scenario 2: Literal Protection
docs.text:
Someone changes "Python 3.11+" to "Python 3.10+" in README.md:

$ docsync check

⚠️  Warning: Protected literal changed
  "Python 3.11+" → "Python 3.10+"
  In: README.md

  This is a protected literal in .docsync/donttouch
  Ensure this change is intentional.

docs.title: List Protected Content
docs.text:
docsync list-protected

Output: 

Protected sections:
  README.md#License
  docs/contributing.md#License
  docs/contributing.md#Code of Conduct
  CLAUDE.md#Terminology (ENFORCED)
  docs/BRANDING.md#Color Palette

Global literals:
  "Python 3.11+"
  "Apache-2.0"
  "#7730E1"

docs.title: JSON Output
docs.text:
$ docsync list-protected --format json
{
  "protected_sections": [
    "README.md#License",
    "docs/contributing.md#Code of Conduct"
  ],
  "protected_literals": [
    "Python 3.11+",
    "Apache-2.0"
  ]
}

docs.title: What to Protect
docs.text:
Do protect: - Legal text (licenses, CoC) - Brand assets (colors, logos) - Version requirements - Enforced terminology - Project policies

Don't protect: - Implementation details - Code examples - API documentation - Tutorials

docs.title: Audit Skill Integration
docs.text:
The docsync audit skill automatically suggests donttouch protections when analyzing your docs:

# In Claude Code
> Run the docsync audit skill

The audit will identify: - License sections - Version requirements - Brand references - Policy sections

And suggest adding them to .docsync/donttouch.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Tutorial - See donttouch in action
  • Skills - Learn about the audit skill

docs.title: Links
docs.text: docsync uses a centralized TOML file to define code-documentation relationships.

docs.title: Why Centralized Links?
docs.text:
No inline comments in code. Instead of polluting source files with # docsync: docs/api.md, all relationships live in .docsync/links.toml.

Benefits:

  • Single source of truth - All links in one place
  • Clean codebase - No documentation metadata in source files
  • Easy auditing - See all relationships at a glance
  • Version controlled - Links are tracked in git
  • Agent-friendly - Machines can parse TOML easily

docs.title: Link Format
docs.text:
Each link defines a code→doc relationship:

[[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["docs/api.md"]

docs.title: Code Field
docs.text:
The code field specifies which source file(s) to track:

Single file: 

code = "src/auth.py"

Glob pattern: 

code = "src/models/*.py"

All Python files in src/models/ are linked to the specified docs.

docs.title: Docs Field
docs.text:
The docs field is an array of documentation targets. Each target can be:

Whole file: 

docs = ["docs/api.md"]

Specific section: 

docs = ["docs/api.md#Authentication"]

Links to the Authentication section (identified by heading).

Multiple targets: 

docs = [
  "docs/api.md#User Endpoints",
  "docs/models.md#User Model",
  "CLAUDE.md#User Management",
]

One code file can link to multiple doc sections.

docs.title: Section-Level Links
docs.text: Section-specific links are more precise than whole-file links:

docs.title: Without Sections
docs.text:
[[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["docs/api.md"]  # 500-line file with 15 sections

Problem: Any change to src/auth.py marks the entire 500-line doc as stale.

docs.title: With Sections
docs.text:
[[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["docs/api.md#Authentication"]  # Specific 44-line section

Solution: Only the Authentication section is marked stale. Other sections remain fresh.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
docsync:

  1. Parses docs/api.md to find the Authentication heading
  2. Extracts the line range (e.g., lines 45-89)
  3. When checking staleness, runs git diff on those specific lines
  4. If lines 45-89 changed → section was updated, not stale
  5. If lines 45-89 unchanged → section is stale

This is accurate: updating a different section doesn't clear staleness for Authentication.

docs.title: Glob Patterns
docs.text:
Use glob patterns to link entire directories:

[[link]]
code = "src/models/*.py"
docs = ["docs/models.md"]

Matches: - src/models/user.py - src/models/post.py - src/models/comment.py

Recursive globs: 

code = "src/**/*.py"

Matches all Python files under src/, recursively.

docs.title: Multiple Links for One File
docs.text:
You can define multiple links for the same code file:

# General API docs
[[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["docs/api.md#Authentication"]

# Implementation details
[[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["docs/architecture.md#Auth Module"]

# Claude Code instructions
[[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["CLAUDE.md#Authentication Patterns"]

When src/auth.py changes, all three docs must be updated.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Check that all link targets exist:

docsync validate-links

Output: 

✓ All 15 links are valid

Or if issues found: 

❌ Invalid links:
  src/auth.py → docs/api.md#NonExistent
    Section 'NonExistent' not found in docs/api.md

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Staleness Detection - How docsync detects stale docs
  • Configuration - Customize link requirements

docs.title: Staleness Detection
docs.text: docsync uses git diff analysis to detect when documentation becomes stale.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: The core insight: If code changed but the linked doc section didn't, the doc is stale.

docs.title: Detection Algorithm
docs.text:
For each code→doc link:

  1. Get the last commit that changed the code file: 

    git log -1 --format=%H -- src/auth.py

  2. Run git diff from that commit to HEAD on the doc file: 

    git diff <commit> HEAD -- docs/api.md

  3. Parse the diff to see which lines changed

  4. Check if changed lines overlap with the linked section's line range

  5. Verdict:

  6. Lines overlap → doc was updated → not stale ✓
  7. No overlap → doc unchanged → stale ❌

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Given this link: 

[[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["docs/api.md#Authentication"]

And this section in docs/api.md: 

## Authentication    ← Line 45

The authentication module handles user login...

(content from lines 45-89)

Scenario 1: Doc was updated

$ git log -1 src/auth.py
commit abc123 "refactor: extract auth helpers"

$ git diff abc123 HEAD -- docs/api.md
@@ -50,3 +50,4 @@
 The authenticate() function now uses:
+- crypto.validate_token() for JWT validation

Result: Lines 50-51 changed (within range 45-89) → not stale ✓

Scenario 2: Doc was NOT updated

$ git diff abc123 HEAD -- docs/api.md
(no output - file unchanged)

Result: No lines changed → stale ❌

docs.title: Section-Level Precision
docs.text: Section-specific links enable targeted staleness detection:

docs.title: Whole-File Link (Imprecise)
docs.text:
[[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["docs/api.md"]

Any change to docs/api.md clears staleness for the entire file. Changing the "Session Management" section clears staleness for the "Authentication" section, even though src/auth.py only relates to authentication.

docs.title: Section Link (Precise)
docs.text:
[[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["docs/api.md#Authentication"]

Only changes to the Authentication section (lines 45-89) clear staleness. Updating other sections doesn't affect this link.

docs.title: Transitive Staleness
docs.text: docsync can detect staleness through import chains.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Given these files:

# src/auth.py
from .crypto import validate_token

def authenticate(token):
    return validate_token(token)

# src/crypto.py
def validate_token(token):
    # ...implementation changed...

And this link: 

[[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["docs/api.md#Authentication"]

Scenario: src/crypto.py changed, but src/auth.py didn't.

Without transitive staleness: - src/auth.py unchanged → docs not stale ✓

With transitive staleness: - src/auth.py imports src/crypto.py - src/crypto.py changed - Therefore docs/api.md#Authentication is stale ❌

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Control transitive depth in pyproject.toml:

[tool.docsync]
transitive_depth = 1  # Check imports 1 level deep
  • transitive_depth = 0 - Disable transitive staleness
  • transitive_depth = 1 - Check direct imports only
  • transitive_depth = 2 - Check imports of imports

Warning

Higher depths increase computation cost. Start with transitive_depth = 1.

docs.title: check (Pre-Commit)
docs.text:
Check staged files for stale docs:

docsync check

Used in pre-commit hooks. Only examines docs linked to staged files.

Fast and focused - doesn't scan the entire repo.

docs.title: list-stale (Audit)
docs.text:
List ALL stale docs regardless of recent changes:

docsync list-stale

Used for periodic audits. Scans the entire repository.

Comprehensive but slower - examines every link.

docs.title: JSON Output
docs.text:
Both commands support --format json for machine consumption:

$ docsync list-stale --format json
{
  "stale": [
    {
      "code_file": "src/auth.py",
      "doc_target": "docs/api.md#Authentication",
      "section": "Authentication",
      "reason": "Section unchanged since src/auth.py changed"
    }
  ]
}

Perfect for AI agents and automation.

docs.title: Deterministic Checks + Probabilistic Updates
docs.text: docsync provides deterministic staleness detection that AI agents use for scoped, probabilistic updates:

docs.title: Layer 1: Deterministic (docsync)
docs.text:
$ git commit -m "refactor: extract auth helpers"

docsync: ❌ commit blocked

Stale documentation detected:
  docs/api.md#Authentication (lines 45-89)
    Code: src/auth.py
    Reason: Section unchanged since src/auth.py changed

Precise and machine-readable. No AI guessing required.

docs.title: Layer 2: Probabilistic (AI Agent)
docs.text:
$ docsync list-stale --format json | ai-agent update-docs

AI gets exact coordinates: 

{
  "doc_target": "docs/api.md#Authentication",
  "section": "Authentication",
  "line_range": [45, 89]
}

The AI's task is scoped precisely: - "Update lines 45-89 in docs/api.md" - "Don't touch other sections" - "The authenticate() function now calls crypto.validate_token()"

Result: Deterministic constraints make probabilistic updates reliable.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Protected Content - Guard critical doc sections
  • CLI Reference - All staleness commands

docs.title: Audit Skill
docs.text: The docsync audit skill analyzes your documentation for trackability and suggests improvements.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The audit skill bridges the gap between "messy existing docs" and "docs that docsync can enforce."

It scores each doc file and section on deterministic verifiability — how well docsync can track and verify the content.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this skill when:

  • Onboarding - After docsync init, before writing links
  • Periodic health checks - Audit documentation coverage quarterly
  • After adding docs - New docs need links.toml entries
  • Messy docs - Documentation feels unstructured or hard to track

docs.title: What It Analyzes
docs.text: The skill examines four dimensions:

docs.title: 1. Structure
docs.text:
Good:

  • Tables with file paths, commands, or config values
  • Code blocks with actual commands or snippets
  • Clear headings creating logical sections
  • Short, factual assertions

Bad:

  • Long prose blocks (>300 words) without structure
  • Flat text with no headings
  • Narrative tutorials (hard to track against code)

docs.title: 2. File References
docs.text:
Good:

  • Backtick-quoted paths: `src/auth.py`
  • Clear code→doc mappings
  • Already in links.toml

Bad:

  • Implicit mentions in prose: "The auth module handles..."
  • References not tracked in links.toml

docs.title: 3. Section Scope
docs.text:
Good:

  • Each section maps to 1-2 code files
  • Clear boundaries between sections
  • Section headings match code concepts

Bad:

  • Section references 7+ files
  • No clear mapping between heading and code
  • Mixed concerns in one section

docs.title: 4. Protected Content
docs.text:
Good:

  • Licenses in donttouch
  • Version requirements protected
  • Brand colors protected
  • Critical terminology enforced

Bad:

  • Unprotected license sections
  • Mutable version constraints
  • No policy enforcement

docs.title: Scoring Rubric
docs.text:
Each doc section receives a score (1-10):

Score Meaning Characteristics 9-10 Highly trackable Tables, code blocks, single-file scope, in links.toml 7-8 Trackable File references present, some structure, could use more links 5-6 Partially trackable Mix of structure and prose, missing links.toml entries 3-4 Poorly trackable Long prose, vague references, no links 1-2 Untrackable Pure narrative, no code references, impossible to verify

docs.title: Example Audit Output
docs.text:
# docs/api.md
  Overall: 6/10 (partially trackable)

  ## Authentication (8/10)
    ✓ Contains code examples
    ✓ References src/auth.py
    ⚠ src/auth.py not in links.toml — SUGGEST ADD

  ## Data Pipeline (3/10)
    ✗ 400 words of prose, no tables or code blocks
    ✗ References 7 code files, none in links.toml
    ✗ No clear single-file scope — consider splitting

  ## License (9/10)
    ✓ Short, assertable content
    ⚠ Not in donttouch — SUGGEST PROTECT

docs.title: Suggestions
docs.text: The audit skill provides actionable suggestions:

docs.title: links.toml Additions
docs.text:
# SUGGESTED: Add to .docsync/links.toml

[[link]]
code = "src/auth.py"
docs = ["docs/api.md#Authentication"]

[[link]]
code = "src/pipeline.py"
docs = ["docs/api.md#Data Pipeline"]

docs.title: donttouch Protections
docs.text:
# SUGGESTED: Add to .docsync/donttouch

# Legal text
README.md#License
docs/contributing.md#Code of Conduct

# Version constraint
"Python 3.11+"

# Brand identity
"#7730E1"

docs.title: Restructuring
docs.text:
SUGGESTED: Restructure docs/api.md#Data Pipeline

Current: 400-word prose block referencing 7 files
Better: Split into subsections, one per module:

  ## Data Pipeline
  ### Ingestion (`src/ingest.py`)
  ### Transform (`src/transform.py`)
  ### Output (`src/output.py`)

Then add section-specific links in links.toml.

docs.title: Usage in Claude Code
docs.text: The audit skill is available as .claude/skills/audit.md.

docs.title: Invoke Explicitly
docs.text: > Run the docsync audit skill on all documentation

docs.title: Invoke Contextually
docs.text:
> I just added docs/tutorial.md, can you check if it's trackable?

Claude will run the audit skill and provide specific recommendations.

docs.title: Ideal Onboarding Flow
docs.text:
# 1. Initialize docsync
docsync init

# 2. Run audit in Claude Code
> Audit my documentation and apply the suggestions

# 3. Fill in convention-based links
docsync bootstrap --apply

# 4. Validate
docsync validate-links

# 5. Check coverage
docsync coverage

# 6. Install enforcement
docsync install-hook

The audit step (step 2) provides the foundation for meaningful links.toml entries.

docs.title: What the Skill Can't Do
docs.text:
The audit skill cannot determine:

  • Correctness - Whether docs accurately describe code behavior
  • Completeness - Whether all code features are documented
  • Clarity - Whether docs are well-written for humans

It only measures trackability - how well docsync can verify freshness.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Tutorial - See audit in action
  • Getting Started - Setup docsync
  • Concepts - Understand links
